ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a mung bean concept but no specific entry for sprouted mung bean (beansprouts). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mung bean sprouts / beansprouts (Vigna radiata, sprouted), raw

classification.processing_level: minimally processed (sprouted — mung beans germinated 2–3 days)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard 1 cup of raw beansprouts weighs approximately 100g. A typical hawker preparation (char kway teow, pad thai, laksa) uses 50–100g of beansprouts per portion.

nutrient_source.title: Mung Bean Sprouts / Beansprouts (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14040017; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F14040017 — Mung Bean Sprouts, raw.

gi_source: GI not applicable — beansprouts have very low digestible starch. The carbohydrate (5.9g/100g) is predominantly from sugars released during germination and dietary fibre from the sprout cell wall.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6mg/100g) and low calorie. The germination process increases vitamin C content substantially compared to dry mung beans. A highly affordable vegetable for daily consumption.
hypertension.guidance: Beansprouts are excellent for patients with hypertension. Very low in sodium and calories. Available year-round at very low cost in Singapore — an accessible daily vegetable for all income levels. Avoid highly salted preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low calorie (30 kcal/100g), low digestible carbohydrate, and useful protein (3.0g/100g). Adding a generous portion of beansprouts to noodle and rice dishes increases meal volume and nutritional value while diluting the carbohydrate density.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beansprouts are ideal for patients with type 2 diabetes as a cheap, widely available, low-calorie vegetable to add to every hawker noodle and rice dish. Requesting extra beansprouts at hawker stalls is one of the simplest diabetes dietary strategies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Good folate content (61mcg DFE/100g) for a low-calorie vegetable. Vitamin C (13.2mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis. The sprouting process increases bioavailability of all micronutrients by reducing phytate content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beansprouts are a useful, cheap, widely available addition to the diet of patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Include daily in hawker meals as a free vegetable addition.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mung bean sprouts (taugeh in Malay) are the sprouted form of whole green mung beans (Vigna radiata), germinated for 2–3 days. The germination process dramatically increases vitamin C (from ~5mg to 13.2mg/100g), folate, and riboflavin while reducing phytates. The resulting sprouts are crisp, white, and mildly flavoured. One of the most affordable, widely available, and popular vegetables in Singapore — found in virtually every hawker noodle dish. Very low in calories (30 kcal/100g) with negligible digestible starch. Used raw or lightly blanched in pad thai, char kway teow, laksa, mee rebus, wonton noodles, popiah, and as a fresh garnish. Distinct from soybean sprouts (larger, yellowish, from germinated soybeans) which have a different nutritional profile.
common_user_queries: mung bean sprouts vs soybean sprouts

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F14040017).
notes: Values per 100g raw mung bean sprouts. Sprouting significantly increases vitamin C compared to dry mung beans and improves overall nutritional bioavailability through phytate reduction. Food safety note: raw sprouts (commercially grown) have occasionally been associated with E. coli and Salmonella contamination — lightly blanching for vulnerable groups (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant) is recommended.
